text: The surgeon begins the incisional hernia repair by lysing adhesions and clearing the fascial margins around the hernia defect, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon carefully lyses difficult and dangerous adhesions, with the small bowel visibly adherent directly to the fascia.
The robotic platform provides enhanced HD-III-D visualization, clearly delineating the tissue planes between the fascia and the intestines.
The surgeon uses sharp scissors without electrocautery to carefully dissect and avoid injury to the intestines.
The surgeon uses a Cartier grasper in the left hand, visible on the left side of the screen, which has a very low grip strength to ensure atraumatic handling of the intestines.
The surgeon uses cautery to address the more vascular fibro fatty adhesions as they move further away from the intestines.
The surgeon begins the repair by performing a primary closure using a V-lock polypropylene suture, visible as it is applied to the tissue.
The surgeon uses a V-lock barbed polypropylene suture, which visibly holds tension securely along its length during the primary closure.
The V-lock suture is shown with a loop on its initial edge, allowing it to be secured to the fascia without the need for knot tying.
The surgeon begins the primary repair by placing a figure-of-eight suture, pulling it through, and securing it to itself using the loop at the end of the V-lock polypropylene suture.

The surgeon performs a mesh repair using a standard laparoscopic approach, sewing the mesh in place around its circumference. Trans-fascial sutures are used to tack the mesh, and primary closure of the defect is initiated.
The surgeon places several throws through the fascia using a needle, then tightens the suture line by pulling the long suture through the muscle.
The surgeon manipulates the suture by pulling it through the muscle on each needle throw, beginning to tighten the suture line visibly.
Carbon dioxide is released to reduce intra-abdominal pressure, allowing the surgeon to approximate the fascial edges in a large defect.
The surgical field shows a loss of ceiling space, reducing the available area for instrument manipulation, making it challenging to perform the operation with low insufflation pressure.
The surgeon places a portion of the suture line and releases intra-abdominal pressure to tighten it, followed by re-insufflation to regain adequate space for continued suturing.
The surgeon progresses along the suture line, using the needle to approximate the fascial edges, ensuring they align closely.
The surgeon uses needle holders to facilitate suturing, passing the needle back and forth between the right and left hands to achieve optimal angles for stitching.
The surgeon tightens the suture line by pulling the muscle edges together, having placed multiple suture passes. Carbon dioxide pressure is released to facilitate the approximation of the edges.

The surgeon focuses on the inferior margin of the defect, working superiorly towards the initial suture line. The defect is being closed by running sutures from both the top and bottom, aiming to meet in the middle.
The surgeon tightens the lower portion of the suture line while the assistant applies external compression to the abdominal wall, aiding in the approximation of the muscle edges.
The surgeon completes the suture line, reaching the superior suture and crossing over the two suture lines to close the defect.

The surgeon demonstrates the tension-holding capability of the V-lock suture without a knot. The needle is carefully removed and placed in the peritoneum to prevent misplacement. The surgeon ties a knot between the superior and inferior suture ends to secure the closure.

The surgeon places a mesh over the primary repair for reinforcement. A purling suture is secured to the superior and inferior portions of the mesh. The suture tails are brought out through the abdominal wall using a suture passer and tied in the subcutaneous space for trans-fascial fixation.
The bedside assistant inserts the suture passer through the abdominal wall, preparing to secure the mesh in place.
Proline sutures are used to hold the mesh against the underside of the fascia. The mesh is then secured around its circumference with absorbable tacks.

The bedside assistant places absorbable tacks around the circumference of the mesh while the primary surgeon holds the mesh in position and indicates the tack placement locations. The mesh can alternatively be sewn in place using robotic suturing techniques.
The surgical team completes the repair by securing the mesh in place, finalizing the reinforcement of the primary repair.
